explanation: The statement "prop_0 when prop_1 (request) and prop_2 (mode)" translates to an implication. It means that if prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 must also be true. This is represented by the LTL formula (prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0.

The statement "prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2" implies that if both prop_1 and prop_2 are true at any given moment, then prop_0 must also be true at that same moment. This is a global implication, meaning it holds for all time points. Therefore, we use the G (globally) operator to assert that the implication ((prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0) is always true.

The phrase 'prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2' implies a universal truth: whenever 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' are simultaneously true, 'prop_0' must also be true. This 'whenever' is best captured by the global operator 'G' (globally). The logical relationship 'if A then B' is represented by the implication 'A -> B'. Therefore, 'globally (if (prop_1 AND prop_2) then prop_0)' translates to G((prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0).

The statement "prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2" means that if prop_1 and prop_2 are true at any point, then prop_0 must also be true at that same point. This is a global implication: G((prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0).
The statement 'prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2' means that if prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 must also be true. This is a direct implication at the current state.

The statement "The sensor shall prop_0 when the prop_1 (request) and the prop_2 (mode)" means that whenever the conditions 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' are simultaneously true, 'prop_0' must also be true at that very same time. The word "shall" implies a global or continuous requirement. Therefore, a global implication 'G(condition -> consequence)' is appropriate. The condition is 'prop_1 AND prop_2', and the consequence is 'prop_0'.

The phrase 'A when B' translates to a global implication 'G(B -> A)'. In this case, 'prop_0' is 'A' and 'prop_1 and prop_2' is 'B'. Therefore, the LTL formula is 'G((prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0)'. This means that it is always the case that if 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' are true, then 'prop_0' must also be true.

The statement "The prop_0 when the prop_1 (request) and the prop_2 (mode)" implies that prop_0 must be true whenever both prop_1 and prop_2 are true. This translates directly to an implication in LTL.

The statement "prop_0 when prop_1 (request) and prop_2 (mode)" implies that prop_0 is true whenever prop_1 and prop_2 are both true. This translates directly to a logical implication where the conjunction of prop_1 and prop_2 is the antecedent, and prop_0 is the consequent. No temporal operators are needed as the condition and effect are simultaneous.

The statement "The prop_0 when the prop_1 (request) and the prop_2 (mode)" implies that if 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' are true, then 'prop_0' must also be true. This translates to a simple implication: (prop_1 AND prop_2) IMPLIES prop_0.
